"I am building a new company. I am also new to a couple of boards with big plans, and very busy punishing myself by learning new sports like tennis, golf, and skiing."

Patrick featured as part of a rotating group of analysts deployed by Sky Sports in recent seasons and typically covered races in North America. She's a prominent figure across the region owing to her standing as one of the most recognisable female racing drivers and an IndyCar race winner.

‌



JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! All the best sports news and much more on our dedicated Facebook page

Despite Patrick's departure, the broadcaster hasn't brought in any fresh faces for the new season. Naomi Schiff is expected to resume her analyst duties following maternity leave which saw her absent for the closing stages of the 2025 campaign.

Article continues below

Presenter Natalie Pinkham is scheduled to return at the Japanese Grand Prix later this month, following a six-month absence after undergoing neck surgery.

Sports.title: Nina Sparks ready to join ranks of British snowboard history makers at Winter Paralympics
Sports.content:
Nina Sparks ready to join ranks of British snowboard history makers at Winter Paralympics

Nina Sparks will become the first woman to represent ParalympicsGB in snowboarding at Milano Cortina 2026

View Image Nina Sparks in ParalympicsGB kit ahead of Milano Cortina 2026 (Image: ©Luc Percival Photography / Paralympics GB)

Nina Sparks will join a growing tradition of making history in British para snowboarding when she becomes the first woman to represent ParalympicsGB in the sport at Milano Cortina.

‌



The 35-year-old from High Wycombe, who has multiple sclerosis, will make her Paralympics debut this March as the only female athlete in a five-strong contingent of British snowboarders heading to Italy.

‌



And while being with the team just feels like the norm for Sparks, the prospect of heading to a first Paralympics is taking a little more time to sink in.

‌



“I’m just living on a cloud at the moment,” she said. “I’ve just come straight off the back of a competition season, straight into finding out about selection. It’s not sunk in at all.

“I was actually in the airport when I found out and I cried. I was with one of my friends who lives in the States and we were just about to part ways, so I needed a reason to cry.

“It’s really weird because I’ve been the only woman on the team since I joined so I’m used to it and I’m quite happy with it. We go to competitions and I go first because I’m the girl and they do girls first - it’s just normal.

‌



“But it’s exciting to make history. Especially in our team, James [Barnes-Miller] made history by being the first para snowboarder and Ollie [Hall] made history by being the first medallist.

“I’ve been training with these guys for the past four years so to join them in making history is cool. But for me, it doesn’t really feel like a big deal.”

It is a natural progression in a sport that is still relatively in its infancy at the Games, with snowboarding first featuring at the Winter Paralympics at Sochi 2014 as part of the para alpine skiing programme, before being contested as its own sport in 2018 and 2022.

‌



For Sparks, who hails from the same county as Stoke Mandeville, the birthplace of the Paralympics, selection for the Games also feels like a natural progression.

She earned her first World Cup podium finish just a month out from Milano Cortina, taking bronze at the Steamboat World Cup in the USA.

“Everything I’d been working on with my coaches around mindset and my actual snowboarding came together at that race. It was such a big deal,” she said.

‌



“It was just like, ‘Oh my God, I’ve done it! I’ve got it together!’ Obviously I got some really good results as well.

“It’s given me a lot of confidence in myself and my abilities. The field was slightly depleted over in the States, so I’m taking it with a pinch of salt, but it’s given me a big confidence boost seeing the time difference off some of the really fast girls.”

Sparks first learned to snowboard aged 13 at her local dry slope, Wycombe Summit, and began on the path to becoming a classified para snowboarder after being diagnosed with MS in 2021.

‌



She competes in the SB-LL2 category for athletes with a lower limb impairment as a result of nerve damage in her right leg.

Now, she will head to a debut Games with aims of showing the world what she can do, with the added bonus of making history as she does.

“I want to go and do some snowboarding that I’m happy with and if it’s good enough for a medal, that would be absolutely incredible,” said Sparks.

Article continues below

“I want to show that I’ve been doing all this hard work for four years so I can put down this performance on the big stage.

“It’s one of the only times that para snowboarding will be broadcast so it will be a chance for people to watch and be like, ‘Oh look there’s Nina, she’s actually legit on TV.’”

Sports.title: 'Centimetres out' - Scottish pair rue fine margins in defeat at prestigious badminton tournament
Sports.content:
'Centimetres out' - Scottish pair rue fine margins in defeat at prestigious badminton tournament

Alexander Dunn and Julie MacPherson were full of belief but fell on the wrong side of a 21-19 21-19 defeat.

View Image Alexander Dunn and Julie Macpherson in action in Birmingham (Image: Badmintonphoto)

Alexander Dunn was left smiling despite suffering a narrow defeat alongside Julie MacPherson at the YONEX All England 2026.

‌



The mixed doubles pairing were taking on defending champions Guo Xin Wa and Chen Fang Hui of China in the second round. Having beaten the sixth seeds in straight games last week, Dunn and MacPherson were full of belief but fell on the wrong side of a 21-19 21-19 defeat.

‌



“We played them last week and fortunately got the better of them, so we came in quite confident,” Dunn, from Bellshill, said.

‌



“But they came out and had changed their game plan a little bit, were a little bit more fired up, so that is why they got the early lead, and we took a little time to adjust to that, but we got our game plan right towards the end. Same as at the end of the second, just a couple of loose shots, maybe centimetres out, that was the difference, particularly at 19-19, it is a bit frustrating.”

In the opening stages, the Scottish duo were staring down a six-point disadvantage, but soon found their groove, knocking off five straight points to retake the lead.

Guo and Chen were never far away, however, and were able to win points at crucial times, eventually closing out the game at the second time of asking. The second was much closer, with the lead only stretching past two points in the closing stages.

‌



Still, Dunn and MacPherson were able to bring the game back and were in touching distance of a decider before the Chinese pair took victory on their third match point.

MacPherson, from Edinburgh said: “We obviously started last week’s match really strong whereas they started today’s match really strong, so we did well to claw it back in the first set and get it back to 19-all. They upped their game a wee bit more. Obviously, it is a more prestigious tournament, they wanted to play well.

“They are defending champions, so they know how to play this hall. We are a bit disappointed not to take the win but obviously pleased with how we managed to play.”

‌



The highlight of the match came when Dunn and MacPherson were aiming to level the match early in the second. At one point, both Guo and Chen were on the floor, but still able to return the shuttle, so Dunn got inventive, much to the delight of the crowd.

He joked: “I hit it through my legs, which is quite bad, I really should just have my racquet in front. The crowd was class. We had a few good rallies in general, and a few of Julie’s shots were incredible, and I actually just came away smiling, I was really pleased.

“We really felt the crowd, and we were gutted we couldn’t take it to a third set at least and give them something to cheer and having that home advantage in the third set might have made the difference.”

Article continues below

Sports.title: Callum Hemming and Estelle van Leeuwen react to YONEX All England defeat
Sports.content:
Callum Hemming and Estelle van Leeuwen react to YONEX All England defeat

Callum Hemming and Estelle van Leeuwen bemoaned simple errors that saw them slip to defeat at the YONEX All England 2026.

View Image Estelle van Leeuwen and Callum Hemming (Image: Badmintonphoto)

Callum Hemming and Estelle van Leeuwen bemoaned simple errors that saw them slip to a 21-14 21-12 defeat at the YONEX All England 2026.

The mixed doubles pairing from Milton Keynes were playing in the first round of their home tournament against the experienced duo Goh Soon Huat and Lai Shevon Jemie of Malaysia. Despite opening up an early lead in the first game, Hemming and Van Leeuwen were unable to stop their opponents coming back into the match, who claimed a comfortable victory.

“I think we started off quite well, and then they reacted to how we came out even better,” Hemming said. “And I don’t think we reacted very well to that. I don’t think we found a good solid rhythm in our rallies today.

“When they went a bit longer, I don’t think the quality was right there and we made a couple too many easy mistakes and allowed them to get confidence. And if you are going to allow a pair that is 11 in the world to grow in confidence then you are going to make your job a lot harder.”

The final English shuttlers left in the draw raced out to a 7-2 lead, winning five consecutive points, but were soon reeled in by their opponents, the world number 11s. Goh and Lai’s speed was hard to live with at times for Hemming and Van Leeuwen, who twice conceded five points in a row, as they went from 16-14 down to losing the opening game 21-14.

The English pair were never ahead in the second game as Van Leeuwen struggled to find her range in the great arena. However, the pair kept pushing and were an over-the-net call away from levelling the contest in the second.

Van Leeuwen said: “I think we didn’t really find any rhythm today, we have a good two or three points but then they would string together a good four or five points or we would make pretty simple errors. You can't do that against a pair that good, it is not going to happen. They are never going to give you an easy, free points and I think we gave them far too many today but that also just shows the level that we need to work to.”

The pair will be back in action next week in the Swiss Open as Van Leeuwen continues her return from a back injury. The pair’s winter training has been impacted by injury, but now back on court they are hopeful to see improvements to their game.

Article continues below

Van Leeuwen added: “We are feeling good, we are obviously gutted that we didn’t play our best in a home tournament with a home crowd but it doesn’t mean that we can’t go out next week at the Swiss Open and put out a good performance against another top pair. We have got good coaches around us now that we think we can do really well with so we will just keep working towards it.”

Sports.title: Paul Townend says Galopin Des Champs could have ‘massive chance’ in open Gold Cup
Sports.content:
Paul Townend says Galopin Des Champs could have ‘massive chance’ in open Gold Cup

The top jockey who has ridden the star to two victories in the Cheltenham Festival’s biggest race is not ruling out a third in next Friday's big race

View 2 Images Paul Townend is not ruling out a third Gold Cup win for Galopin Des Champs

Paul Townend is not ready to write off Galopin Des Champs's chances of winning a third Boodles Cheltenham Gold Cup. The top jockey has ridden the Willie Mullins-trained star to two wins in the famous race and finished second in the race 12 months ago behind Inothewayurthinkin.

‌



He bounced back to capture the Punchestown Gold Cup last April but he has not won a race since. He finished third in the Savills Chase in December and filled the same place behind stablemate Fact To File in February, both races run at Leopardstown which has been a traditional fortress for Galopin Des Champs.

‌



Cheltenham 2026: Willie Mullins drops broad hint about Gold Cup plans READ MORE :

Cheltenham 2026: Willie Mullins talent compared to Faugheen has big race target decided READ MORE :

Fact To File appears set to defend his crown in the Ryanair Chase leaving the Gold Cup wide open. Galopin Des Champs is one of six horses prominent in the betting along with Gaelic Warrior, Jango Baie, The Jukebox Man, Haiti Couleurs and Inothewayurthinkin.

‌



Even though Gaelic Warrior finished ahead of Galopin Des Champs last time and is among the favourites, Townend is highly unlikely to desert the two-time race winner, nor dismiss his chances of making it three wins.

View 2 Images Paul Townend with Galopin Des Champs (Image: PA Archive/PA Images)

‌



''I know Galopin hasn't been at his brilliant best this season, but I don't think he's finished yet by any stretch of the imagination,” said the jockey, Betway’s racing ambassador. “He feels in great form at home, and we're all hoping he'll return to his peak on the biggest stage of all.

''Winning any Gold Cup is a massive achievement, but for him to go there, win a third Gold Cup, and regain his crown would be huge for everybody involved with the horse.

“It's probably the most open Gold Cup I can remember, and there are plenty of horses in there you could make a really good case for, but if Galopin can return to his best, then he'd have a massive chance.”

Article continues below

Sports.title: Joe Calzaghe toasts 20-year anniversary of make-or-break moment that defined career
Sports.content:
Joe Calzaghe toasts 20-year anniversary of make-or-break moment that defined career

Joe Calzaghe is considered as one of the best British boxers if all time but his career could have gone in a different direction had he not beaten Jeff Lacey in Manchester 20 years ago

View 2 Images Calzaghe's win against Lacy was a defining moment (Image: Getty Images)

Every world champion has a rite-of-passage, a fight that changes everything. Not so much how they feel about themselves, but how others see them.

‌



Mine was Juan Laporte in February 1985. Four months later I beat Eusebio Pedroza to lift the coveted WBA featherweight crown, drawing an audience of 19 million on TV.

‌



Joe Calzaghe has been celebrating his defining moment this week, the 20th anniversary of his super-middleweight unification bout against the formidable Jeff Lacy, an unbeaten American giving off Sonny Liston vibes.

‌



I was working in the commentary box that night. I watched him train for a couple days in Wales, interviewed his father and trainer, Enzo. It was clear there was real tension around this fight.

Critics tried to discredit Calzaghe over his punching style. They said he slapped rather than punched. For all that, you wouldn’t want to be slapped by his left hand.

Dana White would accept boxing fight with Eddie Hearn to settle feud READ MORE :

Anthony Joshua chooses his side in Conor Benn vs Eddie Hearn feud READ MORE :

Calzaghe dominated from the off, totally schooled him. Manchester’s MEN Arena was the British boxing Mecca in those days, a 20,000-arena packed to the gunnels.

‌



Calzaghe had already beaten British rival Robin Reid, the teak-tough Omar Sheika, the hard-hitting David Starie and Richie Woodhall to establish himself as the standout on this side of the Atlantic.

It all looked so easy for him. He sprayed you with combinations, not a concussive puncher but spiteful. Everything worked off the southpaw jab. He was afraid of no-one, including Lacey, who was sent to the canvas in the 12th as if to rubber stamp the conquest.

After Lacey, Calzaghe beat the great Dane Mikkel Kessler then topped his career with closing victories over veterans Bernhard Hopkins and Roy Jones Jnr.

‌



View 2 Images Joe Calzaghe dominated Jeff Lacy in Manchester (Image: Getty Images)

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! All the best sports news and much more on our dedicated Facebook page

The Americans finally gave him his due, as they did me in victory over Laporte. I always believed I had the potential to be world champion, but convincing American audiences was a different matter.

‌



I didn’t care that much personally, but some did and it was nice to ram home the point. Laporte had just lost his world title to Wilfredo Gomez. He was still in his pomp, however, and had a good ten years after I beat him.

He caught me with an amazing shot in the ninth, bang on the button. I heard the audience gasp. That shot knocked out most of his opponents but I took it, earning his respect and that of the doubters in the States.

Calzaghe didn’t need any to tell him how good he was. He retired unbeaten, an all-time British great, arguably the best super-middle this country has produced.

Sports.title: Paralympic Games boycott into double figures as UK join growing Russia protest
Sports.content:
Paralympic Games boycott into double figures as UK join growing Russia protest

More than 10 countries, including the UK, have announced a boycott ahead of the 2026 Paralympic Games

View 2 Images The UK will not have any government officials at the 2026 Winter Paralympics opening ceremony (Image: 2026 Getty Images)

The United Kingdom will be one of twelve nations to boycott the Winter Paralympics opening and closing ceremonies. The decision is in protest against Russian and Belarusian athletes being allowed to compete again under their own flags.

‌



Ten athletes from the two countries will be allowed to do so at the Milano-Cortina Winter Paralympic Games, marking the first time since the Russian invasion of Ukraine, with the support of Belarus in February 2022.

‌



It comes after the International Paralympics Committee (IPC) lifted a partial ban and Russia and Belarus successfully appealed to the Court of Arbitration for Sport (CAS) against a ban imposed by FIS, the worldwide governing body for skiing and snowboarding.

‌



JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! All the best sports news and much more on our dedicated Facebook page

Seven Paralympic committees, starting with Ukraine, have announced they will not attend Friday evening's opening ceremony. That includes the athletes and delegations of the Czech Republic, Finland, Poland, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania.

A further five countries, including the UK, will not be sending government ministers or official state representatives as a form of protest. The other countries are France, Germany, the Netherlands and Croatia, with the decision also applying to the closing ceremony on March 15 in Cortina.

‌



British government BOYCOTT Winter Paralympics ceremonies in protest over Russia return READ MORE :

Meanwhile, the separate decision to allow Team GB athletes to skip the ceremony is mainly due to concerns over timing, with the Games starting almost immediately afterwards. The concern is that attending the ceremony would mean late travel, disrupted sleep and reduced recovery time right before the start of events.

Fewer than 60 per cent of countries will therefore send full delegations to the Arena di Verona, both due to logistical reasons and protests. The Winter Paralympics run from March 6 to 15. The closing ceremony of the able-bodied Winter Olympics only finished on February 22.

A spokesperson for the British Government said: "We strongly oppose the decision of the International Paralympic Committee to allow Russian and Belarusian athletes to compete under their own flags at the Milan-Cortina 2026 Paralympic Winter Games.

‌



View 2 Images Russian and Belarusian athletes will be able to compete under their flag (Image: 2026 Getty Images)

"We have been clear that the Russian and Belarusian states should not be represented in international sport whilst the barbaric full-scale invasion of Ukraine is ongoing. Therefore, no Government ministers or officials will attend the opening or closing ceremonies of the Paralympics."

The UK Government statement further noted that Sports Minister Stephanie Peacock would be in Cortina solely to back British athletes. The statement added: "They have worked tirelessly over the last four years to be ready for the biggest moment of their sporting lives and deserve to have the nation behind them."

Article continues below

Meanwhile, French Minister of Sports Marina Ferrari said: "This is a decision we have carefully considered, one that is respectful of the sporting institutions, the IPC, and the decision already made, but it is a disagreement we are expressing with the position that has been adopted."

Sports.title: Lindsey Vonn returns to training just four weeks after horror Winter Olympics crash
Sports.content:
Lindsey Vonn returns to training just four weeks after horror Winter Olympics crash

Lindsey Vonn sustained a broken leg during a brutal crash at the Winter Olympics but it appears that the 41-year-old is keen to get back to work sooner rather than later

View 2 Images Lindsey Vonn shared videos of her in the gym (Image: lindseyvonn/Instagram)

Lindsey Vonn has remarkably returned to training just four weeks after breaking her leg in a horror crash at the Winter Olympics. Vonn, 41, crashed out of her opening downhill attempt in Italy just days after rupturing her cruciate ligament in practice.

‌



She was airlifted to hospital, where she was told she had broken her leg and then underwent corrective surgery. Vonn has now offered a fresh update to her fans following fears that she might have lost her leg.

‌



She took to social media to share videos of herself in the gym where she was shown lifting weights. She also shared one video where she put weight onto her leg.

‌



“Definitely some hard times, but still thankful… still working hard,” Vonn’s caption of the post read. “The only goal is to get healthy. One day at a time. #icandothis.”

Lindsey Vonn says her muscles are 'gone' as true extent of injury hell made clear READ MORE :

Laura Woods slams 'baffling' Winter Olympics star stance as she doubles down in row READ MORE :

JOIN US ON FACEBOOK! All the best sports news and much more on our dedicated Facebook page

NBA icon LeBron James was among those to offer support in response. He wrote: “You Got This!! Small thing to a GIANT!! LFG!!”

‌



Vonn’s latest update comes after she revealed that true mental toll of her devastating injury. Taking to social media last month, she wrote: "Today was a hard day... my physical battle began the second I got hurt but the mental battle started today.

"It hit me like a ton of bricks. It's a battle I'm used to because I've done it so many times. I have always learned from every injury.

"Each one has made me a better and stronger person in different ways... but the battle of the mind can be dark and hard and unrelenting.

‌



View 2 Images Vonn was able to put weight onto her leg (Image: lindseyvonn/Instagram)

"Someone I care about said 'I am a master at the psychological game of life...' I don't know if that's true.... I do know hard days are coming but I will find a way back to the top of the mountain of life."

She added: "I was in the hospital a little longer than I hoped because I had very low haemoglobin from the blood loss from all the surgeries. I was really struggling, the pain was a little bit out of control and I had to have a blood transfusion. That helped me a lot and I turned the corner and now I am out.

‌



"I am in a wheelchair right now, I am very much immobile, and I will be in a wheelchair for a while because I also broke my right ankle.

"I hope I can be on crutches in a little bit but we will see, and I will probably be on crutches for at least two months. But I'll get right to work on rehab and see what I can do and take it one step at a time, like I always do."
